drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus on Day 1, then 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 Days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46–48 Hours) continuous infusion

tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFOX is recommended for adjuvant treatment of stage III pMMR/MSS small bowel adenocarcinoma, or for stage II with high-risk features such as T4, close/positive margins, few lymph nodes examined, tumor perforation, lymphovascular/perineural invasion, or poorly differentiated histology. The role of adjuvant chemotherapy remains controversial, and enrollment in clinical trials is encouraged. Data are extrapolated from colorectal cancer studies, as no patients with SBA were included in the IDEA pooled analysis of adjuvant colon cancer trials. The duration of therapy is typically 3–6 months for CAPEOX or 6 months for FOLFOX, fluorouracil/leucovorin, or capecitabine. For duodenal cancers with positive margin after adjuvant systemic therapy, sequential chemoradiation with capecitabine or infusional fluorouracil may be considered.

drugs.name: Leucovorin
drugs.schedule: 2-hour infusion weekly x 6, repeated every 8 weeks for 4 cycles

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: 1 hour after leucovorin, weekly x 6, repeated every 8 weeks for 4 cycles

tier_1_regimens.notes: Fluorouracil/Leucovorin is an adjuvant treatment option for stage III pMMR/MSS small bowel adenocarcinoma or for stage II with high-risk features. It may also be considered for T3, N0, M0 tumors without high-risk features. The regimen is based on the Roswell Park regimen or weekly dosing as shown. Evidence is extrapolated from colorectal cancer data, and the benefit of adjuvant therapy in SBA is not definitively proven. Participation in clinical trials is preferred.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus followed by 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 Days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46–48 Hours) continuous infusion

tier_1_regimens.notes: sLV5FU2 is a simplified biweekly infusional regimen of fluorouracil and leucovorin used for adjuvant therapy in small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is an alternative to the weekly Roswell Park regimen. The regimen is extrapolated from colorectal cancer data, and its specific efficacy in SBA is not established from dedicated trials. It may be used for stage III disease or high-risk stage II.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: daily on Days 1–5 or 1–7 for 5 weeks with RT

tier_1_regimens.notes: Fluorouracil with radiation therapy (chemo/RT) is considered for duodenal cancers with positive margins after resection, either as adjuvant therapy or for locally unresectable disease following induction chemotherapy. Database analysis suggests no survival benefit from adjuvant chemo/RT versus chemotherapy alone in resected duodenal adenocarcinoma, but it may be considered in selected cases. Preoperative chemo/RT should be considered for patients who remain unresectable after induction chemotherapy. Treatment is delivered using 3D-CRT or IMRT, with fluoropyrimidine-based chemotherapy given concurrently.

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.schedule: twice daily for 14 days every 3 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: CAPEOX is recommended for adjuvant treatment of stage III pMMR/MSS small bowel adenocarcinoma, or for stage II with high-risk features. The duration of therapy is 3–6 months. Evidence is extrapolated from colorectal cancer data; in the IDEA pooled analysis of adjuvant colon cancer trials, 3 months of CAPEOX showed noninferior 3-year disease-free survival compared to 6 months, with less toxicity, but no patients with SBA were included. North American patients may require lower doses of capecitabine due to higher toxicity.

drugs.name: Atezolizumab
drugs.schedule: every 2 weeks for 12 cycles (6 months), then maintenance atezolizumab 840 mg IV every 2 weeks for 13 cycles (12 months total)

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFOX combined with atezolizumab is a new adjuvant treatment recommendation for patients with dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype (e.g., TMB>50 mut/Mb) small bowel adenocarcinoma, specifically for T4, N0, M0 or any T, N1-2 stages. The role of adjuvant immunotherapy for those who received neoadjuvant immunotherapy is not defined. The regimen is based on the ATOMIC trial (Alliance A021502) for dMMR colon cancer, extrapolated to SBA. Enrollment in clinical trials is encouraged.

drugs.name: Atezolizumab
drugs.schedule: every 3 weeks for 8 cycles (6 months), then maintenance atezolizumab 1200 mg IV every 3 weeks for 8 cycles (12 months total)

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: CAPEOX combined with atezolizumab is an adjuvant treatment option for patients with dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype (e.g., TMB>50 mut/Mb) small bowel adenocarcinoma, for T4, N0, M0 or any T, N1-2 stages. Similar to FOLFOX + atezolizumab, it is based on the ATOMIC trial for dMMR colon cancer. The regimen includes capecitabine at 1000 mg/m2 twice daily for 14 days every 3 weeks with oxaliplatin, plus atezolizumab. Participation in clinical trials is preferred.

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.schedule: twice daily for 14 days every 3 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Capecitabine monotherapy is an adjuvant treatment option for stage III pMMR/MSS small bowel adenocarcinoma or for stage II with high-risk features. It is typically given for 6 months. Evidence is extrapolated from colorectal cancer data. For duodenal cancers with positive margin, sequential chemoradiation with capecitabine may be considered after adjuvant systemic therapy. FDA boxed warning: test for DPYD variants before starting capecitabine unless immediate treatment is necessary.

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.schedule: twice daily Monday–Friday, on days of radiation treatment only, throughout the duration of RT (typically 28–30 treatment days)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Capecitabine with radiation therapy (chemo/RT) is used for duodenal cancers with positive margins, either as adjuvant therapy or for locally unresectable disease following induction chemotherapy. It is an alternative to fluorouracil + RT. The regimen is extrapolated from rectal cancer data (e.g., neoadjuvant 5-FU or capecitabine plus radiation). RT is delivered using 3D-CRT or IMRT, and capecitabine is given concurrently at 825 mg/m2 twice daily on days of radiation.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus on Day 1, then 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 Days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46–48 Hours) continuous infusion

tier_1_regimens.notes: Modified FOLFOX6 is a first-line intensive therapy option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma in pMMR/MSS patients. It may be used with or without bevacizumab. Discontinuation of oxaliplatin should be strongly considered after 3 to 4 months of therapy or sooner for unacceptable neurotoxicity, while maintaining other agents until progression. Oxaliplatin may be reintroduced if discontinued for neurotoxicity rather than disease progression. The majority of data on first-line treatment of metastatic SBA are for oxaliplatin-based regimens.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 Days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46–48 Hours) continuous infusion

tier_1_regimens.notes: Modified FOLFOX7 is a variant of FOLFOX without the bolus fluorouracil, used as first-line therapy for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It may be combined with bevacizumab. The regimen is similar to Modified FOLFOX6 but omits the initial bolus dose of fluorouracil, which may reduce toxicity. Evidence is from retrospective studies and extrapolated from colorectal cancer data.

FOLFOX plus bevacizumab is a first-line intensive therapy option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. Bevacizumab has been shown to be safe in advanced SBA, although efficacy has not been proven. It may be added to FOLFOX, CAPEOX, FOLFIRI, or FOLFIRINOX. Bevacizumab may be safely given at a rate of 0.5 mg/kg/min. An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for bevacizumab.

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.schedule: twice daily for 14 days every 3 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: CAPEOX is a first-line intensive therapy option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It may be used with or without bevacizumab. A phase II trial showed an ORR of 50% with 10% complete response in advanced adenocarcinomas of the small bowel and ampulla of Vater. Discontinuation of oxaliplatin after 3–4 months is recommended to limit neurotoxicity. Evidence suggests North American patients may require lower doses of capecitabine due to higher toxicity.

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.schedule: twice daily for 14 days every 3 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: CAPEOX plus bevacizumab is a first-line therapy option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. A phase II study reported that CAPEOX combined with bevacizumab is safe and efficacious in patients with SBA. Bevacizumab is added at 7.5 mg/kg every 3 weeks. The combination may improve outcomes, but data are limited. An FDA-approved biosimilar for bevacizumab is acceptable.

drugs.name: Leucovorin
drugs.route: IV infusion to match irinotecan infusion

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus Day 1, then 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 Days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46–48 Hours) continuous infusion

tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFIRI is a first-line intensive therapy option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It may be used with or without bevacizumab. While FOLFIRI has not been studied as first-line treatment for advanced SBA, it may be a reasonable first-line option based on extrapolation from colorectal cancer studies and its differing toxicity profile compared to oxaliplatin-based regimens. It is also a second-line option if not previously given.

FOLFIRI plus bevacizumab is a first-line option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. Bevacizumab may be added to FOLFIRI as to other regimens, and it has been shown to be safe in advanced SBA, although efficacy is not proven. The combination may be considered for patients with good performance status.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV push Day 1, then 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 Days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46 Hours) continuous infusion

tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFIRINOX is a first-line intensive therapy option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. The role of FOLFIRINOX for SBA has not been formally evaluated, but it is extrapolated from the CAPIRINOX trial in advanced SBA, which showed a response rate of 37.5%. FOLFIRINOX is recommended instead of FOLFOXIRI because FOLFOXIRI uses a higher dose of fluorouracil (3200 mg/m2 over 48 hours), which may be more toxic in U.S. patients. The dose of fluorouracil here is 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours, consistent with FOLFOX or FOLFIRI.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 Days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46 Hours) continuous infusion

tier_1_regimens.notes: Modified FOLFIRINOX is a variant with lower irinotecan dose (150 mg/m2 instead of 180 mg/m2) and no bolus fluorouracil, used as first-line therapy for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It may be combined with bevacizumab. The modification aims to reduce toxicity while maintaining efficacy. Evidence is extrapolated from colorectal cancer studies.

FOLFIRINOX or Modified FOLFIRINOX combined with bevacizumab is a first-line option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. Bevacizumab may be added to these intensive regimens. Data supporting the addition of bevacizumab are limited but suggest safety and possible efficacy based on retrospective studies.

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.schedule: twice daily for 14 days every 3 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Capecitabine monotherapy is a first-line option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma in patients for whom intensive therapy is not recommended (e.g., older patients or those with comorbidities). It may be used with or without bevacizumab. The dose range is 850–1250 mg/m2 twice daily. Evidence is extrapolated from colorectal cancer data.

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.schedule: twice daily for 14 days every 3 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Capecitabine plus bevacizumab is a first-line option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma when intensive therapy is not recommended. The combination is based on data from colorectal cancer (e.g., AVEX trial in elderly patients) and extrapolated to SBA. Bevacizumab may improve outcomes but efficacy in SBA is not proven.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: 1 hour after start of leucovorin, on same days, repeated every 8 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: The Roswell Park regimen of fluorouracil and leucovorin is an option for first-line therapy in metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma when intensive therapy is not recommended. It is a weekly regimen given for 6 weeks every 8 weeks. Evidence is extrapolated from colorectal cancer data.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus followed by 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 Days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46–48 Hours) continuous infusion

tier_1_regimens.notes: sLV5FU2 is a simplified biweekly infusional regimen of fluorouracil and leucovorin used for first-line therapy in metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma when intensive therapy is not recommended. It is an alternative to the weekly Roswell Park regimen and may be better tolerated.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: 1 hour after start of leucovorin, every week

tier_1_regimens.notes: Weekly fluorouracil and leucovorin is a low-dose regimen for first-line therapy in metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma when intensive therapy is not recommended. It is an alternative to bolus or infusional regimens. Evidence is extrapolated from colorectal cancer studies.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: as per bolus or infusional regimen

tier_1_regimens.notes: Bolus or infusional fluorouracil combined with bevacizumab is a first-line option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma when intensive therapy is not recommended. The fluorouracil can be given as bolus or continuous infusion, and bevacizumab is added at 5 mg/kg every 2 weeks. This combination may be used for patients with lower tolerance for intensive regimens.

Irinotecan monotherapy is a second-line or subsequent therapy option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma if not previously given. It may be used with or without bevacizumab. Alternative dosing includes 180 mg/m2 every 2 weeks or 300–350 mg/m2 every 3 weeks. Evidence is limited and extrapolated from colorectal cancer data.

Irinotecan plus bevacizumab is a second-line option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. Alternative dosing includes irinotecan 300–350 mg/m2 every 3 weeks with bevacizumab 7.5 mg/kg every 3 weeks. The combination is based on colorectal cancer data and extrapolated to SBA.

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is a PD-1 inhibitor approved for dMMR/MSI-H or TMB-high (>10 mut/Mb) solid tumors, including metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It can be used as initial therapy or subsequent therapy. In the KEYNOTE-158 study, for 19 patients with SBA, ORR was 42.1% with three complete responses, median PFS 9.2 months, and median duration of response and OS not reached. The TMB-high category was changed from category 2B to category 2A in updates. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab with different dosing.

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab is a PD-1 inhibitor approved for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It can be used as initial therapy or subsequent therapy. In the CheckMate 142 trial for dMMR CRC, ORR was 31.1%, with disease control for at least 12 weeks in 69%. PFS and OS were 50% and 73% at 1 year. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab for monotherapy but not for concurrent use with ipilimumab.

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.schedule: once every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV or 240 mg IV every 2 weeks

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ipilimumab plus nivolumab is a PD-1/CTLA-4 combination immunotherapy for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It can be used as initial therapy or subsequent therapy. In the CheckMate 142 trial, ORR was 55%, disease control for at least 12 weeks was 80%, and PFS and OS were 71% and 85% at 1 year. Nivolumab plus ipilimumab may be considered as subsequent therapy if checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy was previously received.

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cemiplimab-rwlc is a PD-1 inhibitor included for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is based on class effect from data in other cancer types, as it has not been specifically studied in SBA. The dose is 350 mg IV every 3 weeks.

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: Retifanlimab-dlwr is a PD-1 inhibitor included for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is based on class effect from other cancer types. The dose is 500 mg IV every 4 weeks.

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: Tislelizumab-jsgr is a PD-1 inhibitor included for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is based on class effect. The dose is 200 mg IV every 3 weeks.

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: Toripalimab-tpzi is a PD-1 inhibitor included for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is based on class effect. The dose is 3 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks.

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: Penpulimab-kcqx is a PD-1 inhibitor added in updates for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is based on class effect. The dose is 200 mg IV every 2 weeks.

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: Atezolizumab is a PD-L1 inhibitor included for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is based on class effect. Dosing options are 840 mg every 14 days, 1200 mg every 21 days, or 1680 mg every 28 days. Atezolizumab and hyaluronidase-tqjs subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV atezolizumab with different dosing.

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: Avelumab is a PD-L1 inhibitor included for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is based on class effect. The dose is 800 mg IV every 14 days.

drugs.name: Dostarlimab-gxly
drugs.schedule: every 3 weeks for 4 doses, followed by 1000 mg IV every 6 weeks

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: Dostarlimab-gxly is a PD-1 inhibitor approved for dMMR solid tumors, including metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. In the GARNET trial, for 23 patients with small-intestinal solid tumors, ORR was 39.1%, median PFS 8.1 months, and median OS 31.6 months. The regimen is 500 mg IV every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then 1000 mg IV every 6 weeks. It is an option for subsequent therapy if checkpoint inhibitor not previously given.

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cosibelimab-ipdl is a PD-L1 inhibitor added in updates for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is based on class effect. The dose is 1200 mg IV every 21 days.

biomarkers.requirement: deficient mismatch repair or microsatellite instability-high

tier_1_regimens.notes: Durvalumab is a PD-L1 inhibitor added in updates for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is based on class effect. The dose is 1500 mg IV every 28 days.

Albumin-bound paclitaxel (nab-paclitaxel) is a second-line option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. In a phase II trial of 13 patients with SBA, 10 evaluable patients had a disease control rate of 50%, with 2 partial responses and 3 stable disease. Common grade 3 or 4 toxicities included fatigue, neutropenia, and febrile neutropenia.

Docetaxel is a taxane-based chemotherapy option for second-line or subsequent therapy in metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. Evidence is from retrospective studies, showing a 30% disease response rate and median time to progression of 3.8 months in a single-center review.

Paclitaxel is a taxane-based chemotherapy option for second-line or subsequent therapy in metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It can be given every 3 weeks, weekly, or on days 1, 8, 15 every 28 days. Evidence is from retrospective studies and extrapolated from other cancers.

Albumin-bound paclitaxel combined with gemcitabine is a second-line option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. The regimen is based on data from other cancers and extrapolated to SBA. It is given every 28 days.

Docetaxel combined with gemcitabine is a second-line option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. The regimen is given every 21 days, with gemcitabine on days 1 and 8 and docetaxel on day 8. Evidence is extrapolated from other cancers.

Gemcitabine combined with paclitaxel is a second-line option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. The regimen is given every 28 days with both drugs on days 1, 8, and 15. Evidence is extrapolated from other cancers.
Carboplatin combined with paclitaxel is a second-line option for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. Carboplatin is dosed at AUC 5 every 21 days. Evidence is extrapolated from other cancers.

drugs.name: Gemcitabine
drugs.route: IV at a rate of 10 mg/m2/min

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.schedule: twice daily for 14 days every 21 days

tier_1_regimens.notes: GTX regimen is a combination of gemcitabine, docetaxel, and capecitabine used as second-line therapy for metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is given every 21 days for 2–6 cycles. Evidence is limited and extrapolated from other cancers.

Larotrectinib is an NTRK inhibitor approved for NTRK gene fusion-positive solid tumors, including metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. In a pooled analysis, ORR was 75% by independent review. It is well-tolerated with most AEs grade 1 or 2. Used as subsequent therapy if no satisfactory alternative treatments.

trial_data.trial_name: Integrated analysis of three phase I/II studies

tier_1_regimens.notes: Entrectinib is an NTRK inhibitor approved for NTRK gene fusion-positive solid tumors, including metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. In an integrated analysis of 54 patients, ORR was 57%, median PFS 11 months, and median OS 21 months. It is well-tolerated with few discontinuations due to AEs. Used as subsequent therapy if no satisfactory alternative treatments.

drugs.name: Repotrectinib
drugs.schedule: daily for first 14 days, then increase to 160 mg twice daily

tier_1_regimens.notes: Repotrectinib is an NTRK inhibitor approved for NTRK gene fusion-positive solid tumors, including metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. In the TRIDENT-1 trial, for TKI-naïve patients, ORR was 58%, 12-month PFS 56%; for TKI-pretreated, ORR 50%, 12-month PFS 22%. It is used as subsequent therapy if no satisfactory alternative treatments, and shows activity in both NTRK TKI-naïve and pretreated patients.

trial_data.n_patients: 41 efficacy-evaluable, including 1 with small bowel cancer

tier_1_regimens.notes: Selpercatinib is a RET inhibitor approved for RET gene fusion-positive solid tumors, including metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. In the LIBRETTO-001 trial, among 41 efficacy-evaluable patients, ORR was 43.9%, with 2 complete responses (including one small bowel cancer patient). Median PFS was 13.2 months and OS 18 months. Used as subsequent therapy if no satisfactory alternative treatments.

Dabrafenib combined with trametinib is a BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination approved for BRAF V600E-mutated solid tumors, including metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. In the ROAR trial, for 3 patients with small intestine adenocarcinoma, ORR was 67%, median duration of response 7.7 months, and OS 21.8 months. It is used as subsequent therapy if no satisfactory alternative treatments.

Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki is an HER2-directed antibody-drug conjugate approved for HER2-overexpressing (IHC 3+) solid tumors, including metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. In the DESTINY-PanTumor02 trial, ORR was 37.1% overall, with higher ORR in HER2 IHC 3+ tumors. It is used for previously treated patients and/or those with no available alternative treatment options. May not be indicated in patients with underlying lung issues due to lung toxicity (3.5% report of drug-related deaths from interstitial lung disease).

drugs.name: Trastuzumab
drugs.dose: 8 mg/kg IV loading dose on Day 1 of cycle 1, followed by 6 mg/kg IV every 21 days

drugs.name: Pertuzumab
drugs.dose: 840 mg IV loading dose on Day 1 of cycle 1, followed by 420 mg IV every 21 days

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pertuzumab combined with trastuzumab is a dual HER2-targeted therapy for HER2 overexpression/amplification and RAS/BRAF wild-type metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is an option for second-line and subsequent therapy. Evidence is extrapolated from colorectal cancer data (e.g., MyPathway study).

drugs.name: Trastuzumab
drugs.dose: 8 mg/kg IV loading dose on Day 1 of cycle 1, followed by 6 mg/kg IV every 21 days

tier_1_regimens.notes: Tucatinib combined with trastuzumab is a HER2-targeted therapy for HER2 overexpression/amplification and RAS/BRAF wild-type metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is an option for second-line and subsequent therapy. Evidence is extrapolated from colorectal cancer data (e.g., MOUNTAINEER study).

drugs.name: Cetuximab
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV followed by 250 mg/m2 IV weekly

tier_1_regimens.notes: Encorafenib combined with cetuximab is a BRAF inhibitor plus EGFR inhibitor therapy for BRAF V600E-mutated metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is an option for second-line and subsequent therapy. Evidence is from colorectal cancer studies (e.g., BEACON CRC trial) and extrapolated to SBA.

Encorafenib combined with panitumumab is a BRAF inhibitor plus EGFR inhibitor therapy for BRAF V600E-mutated metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is an option for second-line and subsequent therapy. Evidence is from colorectal cancer studies and extrapolated to SBA.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus on Day 1, followed by 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 Days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46–48 hours) IV continuous infusion

tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFOX combined with encorafenib and cetuximab is a triplet regimen for BRAF V600E-mutated metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is an option for subsequent line therapy if no previous treatment with oxaliplatin or BRAF-targeting regimen. The regimen includes encorafenib, cetuximab, and FOLFOX. Evidence is from colorectal cancer studies and extrapolated to SBA.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus on Day 1, followed by 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 Days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46–48 Hours) IV continuous infusion

tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFOX combined with encorafenib and panitumumab is a triplet regimen for BRAF V600E-mutated metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma, similar to the cetuximab version. It is an option for subsequent line therapy if no previous treatment with oxaliplatin or BRAF-targeting regimen. Evidence is from colorectal cancer studies and extrapolated to SBA.

Adagrasib is a KRAS G12C inhibitor approved for KRAS G12C-mutated solid tumors, including metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is used as monotherapy or in combination with cetuximab or panitumumab. Evidence is from colorectal cancer studies and extrapolated to SBA. The dose is 600 mg PO twice daily.

Sotorasib is a KRAS G12C inhibitor approved for KRAS G12C-mutated solid tumors, including metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is used as monotherapy or in combination with cetuximab or panitumumab. Evidence is from colorectal cancer studies and extrapolated to SBA. The dose is 960 mg PO once daily.

Adagrasib combined with cetuximab is a KRAS G12C inhibitor plus EGFR inhibitor therapy for KRAS G12C-mutated metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is an option for second-line and subsequent therapy. Evidence is from colorectal cancer studies and extrapolated to SBA.

Adagrasib combined with panitumumab is a KRAS G12C inhibitor plus EGFR inhibitor therapy for KRAS G12C-mutated metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is an option for second-line and subsequent therapy. Evidence is from colorectal cancer studies and extrapolated to SBA.

Sotorasib combined with cetuximab is a KRAS G12C inhibitor plus EGFR inhibitor therapy for KRAS G12C-mutated metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is an option for second-line and subsequent therapy. Evidence is from colorectal cancer studies and extrapolated to SBA.

Sotorasib combined with panitumumab is a KRAS G12C inhibitor plus EGFR inhibitor therapy for KRAS G12C-mutated metastatic small bowel adenocarcinoma. It is an option for second-line and subsequent therapy. Evidence is from colorectal cancer studies and extrapolated to SBA.

tier_2_handbook.definition: Small Bowel Adenocarcinoma (SBA) is a primary malignant neoplasm arising from the glandular epithelium of the small intestine. The small intestine is anatomically divided into the duodenum, jejunum, and ileum, which originate embryologically from the midgut (except for the first portion of the duodenum, which derives from the foregut). SBA is a relatively rare malignancy, accounting for approximately 30% to 40% of all small intestinal cancers, and comprises only about 3% of gastrointestinal tract cancers [1, 2]. The median age at diagnosis is 66 years, with a roughly equal sex distribution [2]. While the majority of SBA cases arise in the duodenum (52%–58%), the remainder develop in the jejunum (15%–29%), ileum (10%–13%), or an unspecified location (4%–16%) [3, 4]. The disease often presents at a more advanced stage and higher grade compared to colorectal cancer (CRC), and common clinical presentations include complications such as gastric outlet obstruction (for duodenal primaries) or cramping abdominal pain (for jejunal/ileal primaries) [3, 5]. Due to its rarity and often advanced presentation at diagnosis, SBA carries a generally poor prognosis, with a 5-year relative survival rate of 42% for distant metastatic disease compared to 85% for localized disease [6]. The cornerstone of curative-intent treatment for localized disease is surgical resection with en bloc removal of regional lymph nodes [7].

subtypes.name: Adenocarcinoma, pMMR/MSS
subtypes.description: This is the most common subtype, characterized by proficient DNA mismatch repair (pMMR) and microsatellite stability (MSS). These tumors lack the high degree of genomic instability seen in the MSI-H subtype. The NCCN guidelines differentiate treatment pathways for localized (pMMR/MSS) and metastatic disease based on this biomarker status. For localized, resectable disease, primary treatment is surgical resection. Adjuvant therapy recommendations for pMMR/MSS tumors are based on stage and high-risk features, with options including observation, fluoropyrimidine monotherapy, or oxaliplatin-based combination therapy like FOLFOX or CAPEOX [8, 9].
subtypes.frequency: Represents the majority of SBA cases. The exact percentage is not specified in the provided text, but genomic profiling indicates that 49% (n=156 of 317) of SBA samples in one study were MSS, suggesting pMMR/MSS is common [10].
subtypes.key_associations: This subtype is associated with sporadic cases and some hereditary syndromes. Unlike dMMR/MSI-H tumors, pMMR/MSS SBA does not typically derive direct benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy. Genetically, these tumors often harbor KRAS and TP53 mutations but less commonly APC mutations compared to CRC [10].

subtypes.name: Adenocarcinoma, dMMR/MSI-H
subtypes.description: These tumors are deficient in mismatch repair (dMMR) proteins or exhibit high microsatellite instability (MSI-H). This status is a critical prognostic and predictive biomarker. For localized, resectable disease, the primary treatment is resection; for T4 or bulky primaries, neoadjuvant checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy may be considered [11]. For adjuvant treatment of stage II (T4, N0, M0) or stage III (Any T, N1-2) disease, options include FOLFOX + atezolizumab or CAPEOX + atezolizumab, in addition to standard chemotherapy regimens [12]. For metastatic or recurrent disease, checkpoint inhibitor therapy is a key treatment option, either as a first-line intensive therapy alternative or in any subsequent line for patients not previously treated with a checkpoint inhibitor [13, 14].
subtypes.frequency: SBA has a higher incidence of dMMR/MSI-H tumors compared to CRC. One prospective analysis reported that 9.5% (n=30 of 317) of SBA samples had a tumor mutational burden (TMB) of >20 mut/Mb, which is significantly higher than the 4.3% rate in CRC (P=.001) [10]. MMR/MSI testing is recommended for all newly diagnosed SBA patients.
subtypes.key_associations: Strongly associated with Lynch syndrome (hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer, HNPCC), an autosomal dominant condition caused by germline mutations in MMR genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2). Patients with Lynch syndrome have an estimated lifetime risk of 4% for developing SBA, representing a relative risk of >100 compared to the general population [15, 16]. This subtype is also associated with ultra-hypermutated phenotypes from POLE/POLD1 mutations, which are also eligible for checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy [17].

subtypes.name: Adenocarcinoma, POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype
subtypes.description: A distinct molecular subgroup characterized by mutations in the POLE or POLD1 genes, leading to an ultra-hypermutated phenotype, typically defined as tumor mutational burden (TMB) >50 mut/Mb. These tumors are highly immunogenic. The NCCN guidelines include this group alongside dMMR/MSI-H for key treatment decisions, particularly the use of checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy for both localized and metastatic disease [11, 12, 13].
subtypes.frequency: POLE mutation incidence in SBA is reported as 0.3% in one analysis, which was the same as the incidence found in CRC [10].
subtypes.key_associations: Like dMMR/MSI-H tumors, POLE-mutant tumors exhibit high TMB and are predicted to respond well to immune checkpoint inhibitors. The guidelines explicitly recommend considering neoadjuvant or adjuvant checkpoint inhibitor therapy for this group [11, 12].

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The molecular pathogenesis of SBA involves distinct genomic alterations that differentiate it from colorectal and gastroesophageal cancers. Key driver mutations include KRAS and TP53, which are frequent in both SBA and CRC. However, APC mutations are significantly less common in SBA compared to CRC (27% in SBA vs. 76% in CRC; P < .001), suggesting a unique neoplastic transformation pathway [10]. Genomic profiling has identified that SMAD4 and CDKN2A mutations are more common in SBA than in gastroesophageal cancers or CRC [10]. While BRAF mutations occur at a rate similar to CRC, only 10% of BRAF-mutant SBAs have a V600E alteration, compared to >70% in BRAF-mutant CRC [10]. Importantly, biomarkers relevant to targeted and immunotherapeutic treatments are enriched in SBA compared to CRC. These include a higher incidence of MSI-H/dMMR, higher rates of programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) expression, and higher tumor mutational burden-high (TMB-H). One study reported that 9.5% of SBA samples had a TMB of >20 mut/Mb compared to 4.3% in CRC (P = .001) [10, 18, 19]. Human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) alterations, RET fusions, NTRK 1/2/3 fusions, and KRAS G12C mutations are also actionable targets identified in SBA [14, 20, 21]. The presence of these alterations provides the rationale for a biomarker-driven approach to systemic therapy, including the use of checkpoint inhibitors for dMMR/MSI-H or ultra-hypermutated tumors, and targeted therapies for specific mutations.

epidemiology.incidence: Small bowel cancers are relatively rare, accounting for approximately 3% of gastrointestinal tract cancers. In 2024, an estimated 12,440 new cases of small bowel cancer were projected to occur in the United States [1, 2]. Within this category, adenocarcinomas comprise an estimated 30% to 40% of small intestinal cancer diagnoses [2]. The age-adjusted incidence is reported as 2.6 per 100,000 for males and 2.0 per 100,000 for females [2].
epidemiology.mortality: In 2024, an estimated 2,090 patients in the United States were projected to die from small bowel cancer [1].
epidemiology.trends: The incidence of small bowel cancers is increasing, with an annual percent increase of 1.8% between 2006 and 2015. This upward trend contrasts with the decreasing incidence of other gastrointestinal malignancies, including esophageal, gastric, colon, and rectal cancers, over the same timeframe [2].
epidemiology.demographics: The median age at diagnosis is 66 years [2]. The disease affects males and females relatively equally [2]. Most cases of SBA arise in the duodenum (52%–58%), with the remainder in the jejunum (15%–29%), ileum (10%–13%), or unspecified locations (4%–16%) [3, 4]. Patients with SBA tend to be diagnosed at a younger age and often present with a higher stage and grade compared to those with CRC [5]. For localized disease, the 5-year relative survival rate is 85%, while for distant metastatic disease (stage IV), it drops significantly to 42% [6]. Approximately 32% of patients are diagnosed with stage IV disease [6].

risk_factors.detail: An autosomal dominant condition resulting from germline mutations in DNA mismatch repair (MMR) genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2). Carriers have a significantly elevated lifetime risk of developing SBA.
risk_factors.strength: Strong. Lifetime risk of SBA is estimated at 4%, representing a relative risk of >100 compared to the general population [15, 16].

risk_factors.detail: An autosomal dominant condition characterized by a germline APC gene mutation, leading to numerous adenomatous polyps in the large bowel. The duodenum and periampullary region are the most common sites for SBA in these patients.
risk_factors.strength: Strong. The lifetime risk of developing SBA has been estimated at 3% to 5% [22, 23].

risk_factors.detail: An autosomal dominant condition mainly caused by STK11 (LKB1) gene mutations, characterized by hamartomatous and adenomatous gastrointestinal polyps. SBA can arise from either type of polyp.
risk_factors.strength: Very strong. The relative risk of developing SBA is estimated at 520 compared to the general population, with a lifetime risk between 1.7% and 13% [24, 25, 26].

risk_factors.detail: Both ulcerative colitis and Crohn’s disease are recognized risk factors. IBD increases the risk of both colorectal and small bowel cancers.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate to Strong. A retrospective cohort study found the relative risk of small bowel cancer in IBD patients was 3.70 (95% CI, 1.23–11.13) [27, 28, 29, 30, 31].

risk_factors.detail: An immune reaction to gluten that damages the small intestine. The link to SBA is less understood than the link between IBD and CRC.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate. A cohort study found 0.06% of patients with celiac disease were diagnosed with SBA within 1 year of diagnosis, suggesting a possible association [32].

risk_factors.detail: A systematic review reported that high levels of alcohol consumption may increase the risk of SBA. Pooled data showed a non-significant trend towards increased risk with high alcohol consumption.
A systematic review identified smoking as a factor that may increase SBA risk, though a pooled analysis did not identify it as a risk factor.
Low intake of fiber and high intake of red/processed meat and sugary drinks have been reported to increase the risk of SBA in a systematic review.
A pooled analysis showed elevated BMI was associated with a non-significant trend towards increased SBA risk.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Small bowel adenocarcinoma (SBA) often presents with a complication of the tumor rather than early, localized symptoms. The most common presenting symptom is abdominal pain, frequently described as cramping and associated with a partial or complete small bowel obstruction. For duodenal tumors, this may manifest as gastric outlet obstruction with nausea, vomiting, and early satiety. A significant proportion of patients (approximately 25% to 33%) present with occult gastrointestinal bleeding, which can lead to iron-deficiency anemia and fatigue. Other presenting complaints include unexplained weight loss, a palpable abdominal mass, or jaundice specifically for tumors of the second portion of the duodenum that obstruct the ampulla or distal bile duct. The diagnosis is often delayed because symptoms are nonspecific and can mimic more common benign gastrointestinal conditions, leading to a higher stage at diagnosis compared to colorectal cancer [SBA-A 1 of 2, Discussion MS-5].

symptoms.name: Abdominal pain
symptoms.detail: Often cramping in nature, may be intermittent if partial obstruction is present.
symptoms.alarm: Persistent or worsening pain suggests obstruction or locally advanced disease.

symptoms.name: Nausea and vomiting
symptoms.detail: May indicate gastric outlet obstruction (duodenal tumors) or high-grade small bowel obstruction.
symptoms.alarm: Vomiting of bilious material or feculent vomiting suggests significant obstruction.

symptoms.name: Occult gastrointestinal bleeding / Anemia
symptoms.detail: Bleeding can be chronic and occult, leading to iron-deficiency anemia, fatigue, and pallor.
symptoms.frequency: Approximately one-quarter to one-third of cases [Discussion MS-5].

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unintentional weight loss may be due to malabsorption, anorexia, or advanced disease.
symptoms.alarm: Significant, unexplained weight loss (>5% body weight over 6-12 months).

symptoms.name: Jaundice
symptoms.detail: Specifically for ampullary or periampullary duodenal tumors causing biliary obstruction.
symptoms.frequency: Occurs in a subset of duodenal cancers.
symptoms.alarm: Painless jaundice suggests malignant biliary obstruction.

signs.name: Palpable abdominal mass
signs.detail: May be felt on physical examination, particularly in advanced or bulky disease.
signs.frequency: Less common at initial presentation than obstructive symptoms.

signs.name: Signs of anemia
signs.detail: Pallor, tachycardia, and fatigue from chronic blood loss.
signs.frequency: Common, often associated with occult GI bleeding.

signs.name: Signs of obstruction
signs.detail: Abdominal distension, tympany, and hyperactive or hypoactive bowel sounds.

atypical_or_missed: Asymptomatic or incidental findings on imaging or endoscopy performed for other reasons.
Vague, intermittent epigastric discomfort attributed to indigestion or gastritis.
Presentations mimicking inflammatory bowel disease or celiac disease, especially in the context of Crohn's disease or celiac disease-associated SBA.
Iron-deficiency anemia in an older adult without obvious GI source, which should prompt evaluation of the small bowel.
Tumors in the jejunum or ileum that do not cause obstruction until late in the disease course, leading to significant diagnostic delay.

red_flags: Symptoms of bowel obstruction (persistent vomiting, abdominal distension, obstipation).
Clinical signs of significant gastrointestinal bleeding (hemodynamic instability, orthostatic symptoms, melena).
Unexplained significant weight loss in an older adult.
New-onset iron-deficiency anemia without a clear source in the upper or lower GI tract.
Fever or signs of sepsis suggesting tumor perforation or abscess.

diagnostic.test: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis
diagnostic.rationale: Primary imaging modality for evaluating the extent of local tumor infiltration, assessing for lymphadenopathy, and detecting distant metastatic disease (liver, peritoneum). Should be performed with both IV and oral contrast [SBA-A 1 of 2].
diagnostic.key_findings: Identifies mass lesions, bowel wall thickening, obstruction, and metastatic deposits.

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for complete staging to evaluate for pulmonary metastases [SBA-1].
Essential for diagnosis and tissue sampling when a duodenal malignancy is suspected. Provides histologic confirmation [SBA-1, SBA-A 2 of 2].
diagnostic.key_findings: Direct visualization and biopsy of duodenal masses or suspicious lesions.

diagnostic.rationale: Useful for enhanced pre-therapeutic clinical staging of proximal small intestinal malignancies and to discern duodenal from ampullary, biliary, or pancreatic primaries [SBA-1, SBA-A 2 of 2, Discussion MS-5].
diagnostic.key_findings: Assesses depth of tumor invasion (T stage) and regional lymph node involvement (N stage).

diagnostic.test: Push- or device-assisted enteroscopy (double- or single-balloon)
diagnostic.rationale: Not required for routine staging, but may be considered for patients with suspected small bowel strictures for diagnostic and/or palliative benefit. Biopsy can be performed during the procedure [SBA-A 2 of 2].
diagnostic.key_findings: Direct visualization and biopsy of mid or distal small bowel lesions not accessible by EGD.

diagnostic.rationale: Consider when radiographic imaging and other forms of endoscopy fail to reveal a suspected primary lesion. Not the preferred primary method due to inability to obtain tissue. Contraindicated if small bowel obstruction or strictures exist [SBA-A 2 of 2, Discussion MS-5].
diagnostic.key_findings: Identifies mucosal lesions, but cannot provide histology.

staging.test: Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
staging.rationale: Standard for initial staging to evaluate local extent and distant metastases to lungs, lymph nodes, liver, peritoneum [SBA-A 1 of 2].
staging.indication: All patients with confirmed or suspected SBA.

staging.rationale: May be considered where there is contraindication to IV iodinated contrast. MR enterography may improve visualization [SBA-A 1 of 2].
staging.indication: Patients with contraindications to CT contrast, or for further evaluation of indeterminate liver lesions.

staging.rationale: May be obtained in the initial workup of suspected duodenal malignancies to ascertain tumor site of origin, particularly in cases of biliary obstruction [SBA-A 1 of 2].
staging.indication: Duodenal tumors near the ampulla with suspected biliary involvement.

staging.rationale: Not routinely indicated as efficacy and clinical benefit have not been examined compared to CT or MR [SBA-A 1 of 2]. May be considered in instances of equivocal CT or MR results, including the evaluation of potential peritoneal disease, particularly where lesions are sized greater than the lower limits of FDG-PET detection.
staging.indication: Equivocal findings on conventional imaging; evaluation of suspected recurrent disease with rising tumor markers.

biomarkers.rationale: Tumor marker that may be elevated. Used in conjunction with CA 19-9 for baseline assessment and post-treatment surveillance.
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis, prior to treatment initiation, and periodically during surveillance.

biomarkers.rationale: Tumor marker that may be elevated, particularly in duodenal or ampullary cancers. Used with CEA for baseline and surveillance.
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis, prior to treatment initiation, and periodically during surveillance.

biomarkers.test: Mismatch Repair (MMR) or Microsatellite Instability (MSI) Testing
biomarkers.rationale: Universal testing is recommended for all newly diagnosed patients with SBA. Incidence of dMMR/MSI-H is enriched in SBA compared to colon and rectal cancer, making this an important prognostic and/or predictive biomarker for potential benefit from immunotherapy.
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis, on biopsy or resection specimen.
biomarkers.mandatory: Yes, part of recommended workup [SBA-1, SBA-B].

biomarkers.rationale: Testing should be conducted as part of MGPT to identify rare and actionable mutations and gene fusions such as POLE/POLD1, RET, NTRK 1/2/3, BRAF, HER2 (ERBB2) overexpression/amplification, and tumor mutational burden (TMB). Crucial for guiding systemic therapy in the metastatic setting.
biomarkers.when: Recommended as rapidly as possible following diagnosis of metastatic or recurrent disease [SBA-8].
biomarkers.mandatory: Recommended for all patients with metastatic or recurrent disease.

do_not_miss: Universal MMR/MSI testing on all newly diagnosed SBA to identify dMMR/MSI-H status and potential Lynch syndrome.
Genetic counseling and risk assessment for familial syndromes (Lynch, FAP, PJS) in all SBA patients [SBA-1 footnote f].
Biomarker testing (MGPT) for actionable alterations in the metastatic setting (e.g., BRAF V600E, HER2, NTRK, RET, KRAS G12C) to guide therapy.
DPYD testing prior to initiating fluoropyrimidine-based therapy as per updated guidance [SBA-1 footnote b].
avoid: Routine FDG-PET/CT for initial staging in the absence of equivocal CT/MR findings [SBA-A 1 of 2, SBA-6 footnote p].
Capsule endoscopy as the primary diagnostic method due to inability to biopsy. Contraindicated in known obstruction.
Cetuximab or panitumumab for treatment of SBA based on lack of efficacy data in this disease [Discussion MS-12].

staging.system: AJCC 8th Edition (2017) TNM Staging System for Small Intestine Adenocarcinoma.

t_stages.description: Tumor invades the lamina propria or submucosa.
Tumor invades through the muscularis propria into the subserosa, or extends into nonperitonealized perimuscular tissue (mesentery or retroperitoneum) without serosal penetration.

Tumor perforates the visceral peritoneum or directly invades other organs or structures (e.g., other loops of small intestine, mesentery of adjacent loops of bowel, and abdominal wall by way of serosa; for duodenum only, invasion of pancreas or bile duct).

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed.
Metastasis in one or two regional lymph nodes.
Metastasis in three or more regional lymph nodes.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: High-grade dysplasia or carcinoma in situ confined to the epithelium.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly stated; excellent prognosis with local treatment.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor invades lamina propria, submucosa, or muscularis propria but not beyond. No lymph node or distant metastasis.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Localized disease has 5-year relative survival of approximately 85% [Discussion MS-10].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative. Primary treatment is surgical resection.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor invades through muscularis propria into subserosa or nonperitonealized perimuscular tissue. No lymph node or distant metastasis.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Prognosis worse than stage I; survival data not explicitly given.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative. Surgery is primary. Adjuvant therapy may be considered based on risk features.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor perforates serosa or invades adjacent organs/structures. No lymph node or distant metastasis.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Prognosis worse than stage IIA; survival data not explicitly given.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative. Surgery is primary. Adjuvant therapy is commonly recommended.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor of any depth, with metastasis in 1-2 regional lymph nodes. No distant metastasis.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Stage III disease has significantly worse outcomes compared to earlier stages [Discussion MS-6].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative with multimodal therapy. Adjuvant chemotherapy is recommended.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor of any depth, with metastasis in ≥3 regional lymph nodes. No distant metastasis.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Poor prognosis; survival data not explicitly given.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative with multimodal therapy. Adjuvant chemotherapy is recommended.

stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 5-year relative survival of only approximately 42% for stage IV disease [Discussion MS-10].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative. Treatment with systemic therapy; curative intent only in highly selected cases with resectable metastases.

staging_pearls: Regional lymph node stations differ by primary site: Duodenum nodes include retropancreatic, hepatic artery, inferior pancreaticoduodenal, and superior mesenteric nodes. Jejunum/Ileum nodes include cecal (terminal ileum only), ileocolic (terminal ileum only), superior mesenteric, mesenteric, and NOS nodes [SBA-B, SBA-C].

A minimum lymph node evaluation of 8 nodes is recommended by AJCC, though some data suggest higher numbers (e.g., ≥9 for jejunal/ileal tumors) may improve prognostic accuracy [SBA-B, Discussion MS-6].

Pathologic stage reporting should include: primary site, grade, T stage, N status (number of nodes evaluated and positive), margin status (proximal, distal, radial/mesenteric, uncinate, bile duct, pancreas), lymphovascular invasion, MSI/MMR status, and evidence of celiac or Crohn's disease [SBA-B].

For duodenal cancers, differentiation from ampullary malignancy is critical. The epicenter of the tumor should not be at the ampulla, and >75% of the mass should not be within the ampulla for it to be classified as SBA [SBA-B].
Tumor location (duodenum vs. jejunum/ileum) is not part of the AJCC TNM staging but is an important prognostic factor and influences surgical and adjuvant therapy decisions [SBA-2, SBA-4].

management_principles.overview: The NCCN Guidelines for Small Bowel Adenocarcinoma (SBA) emphasize a multimodal, multidisciplinary approach due to the rarity and complexity of this disease. Participation in clinical trials is strongly encouraged for all stages, as high-level evidence is limited. Treatment recommendations are often extrapolated from colorectal cancer (CRC) data. All recommendations are Category 2A (uniform consensus based on lower-level evidence) unless otherwise noted [SBA-1, SBA-2].

by_intent.approach: Primary treatment is surgical resection with en bloc removal of regional lymph nodes. Adjuvant chemotherapy is considered based on pathologic stage and risk factors. For locally advanced or unresectable disease, conversion to resectable status via neoadjuvant therapy is a goal. A multidisciplinary team (MDT) evaluation is crucial for treatment planning, especially for duodenal tumors where chemoradiotherapy may be considered [SBA-2, SBA-4, SBA-6, SBA-7].

by_intent.patient_group: Locally unresectable/medically inoperable or metastatic (Stage IV) disease
by_intent.approach: Treatment goals are prolonging survival, controlling symptoms, and maintaining quality of life. Options include systemic therapy (chemotherapy, immunotherapy), palliative procedures (diversion, stenting) for obstruction, and clinical trials. Multidisciplinary evaluation is recommended to assess for potential conversion to resectable disease or for palliative interventions [SBA-3, SBA-5, SBA-8].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: The NCCN Panel strongly recommends multidisciplinary team evaluation for all patients with SBA. This is particularly critical for: 1) duodenal tumors when neoadjuvant or adjuvant chemoradiotherapy is being considered, 2) cases of locally advanced or unresectable disease to assess resectability and conversion potential, 3) selection for complex surgical procedures like pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple), and 4) management of peritoneal metastases where cytoreductive surgery/HIPEC may be considered (though HIPEC is not recommended outside of clinical trials) [SBA-E, MS-9, MS-10].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: A patient's performance status (PS) is a critical determinant for treatment selection, especially regarding intensive versus non-intensive systemic therapy for advanced disease. While the guidelines do not specify a universal PS cutoff, they state: For initial therapy of metastatic SBA where intensive therapy is not recommended (e.g., older patients), a geriatric assessment should be completed to aid in predicting treatment risks. Non-intensive options like fluorouracil/leucovorin or capecitabine ± bevacizumab are preferred over more toxic regimens like FOLFOX, CAPEOX, or FOLFIRINOX [SBA-D 1 of 12, footnote h].

nodes.criteria: T3, N0, M0 (Stage IIA) with high-risk features OR T4, N0, M0

adjuvant_logic.summary: High-risk features for Stage II: close or positive margins, <5 LN examined (duodenum) or <8 LN examined (jejunum/ileum), tumor perforation. Lymphovascular/perineural invasion or poor histology may also be considered based on CRC data. For duodenal cancer with positive margin post-resection, consider sequential chemo/RT with capecitabine or infusional fluorouracil (SBA-E).

nodes.criteria: T3, N0, M0 (Stage IIA) without high-risk features

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy may be considered based on extrapolation from CRC data.

nodes.criteria: T any, N1-2 (Stage III)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy recommended. For duodenal cancer with positive margin post-resection, consider sequential chemo/RT with capecitabine or infusional fluorouracil (SBA-E).

management_pathways.title: Adjuvant Treatment for Duodenum or Jejunum/Ileum (dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1)

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy recommended for Stage I and Stage II (dMMR/MSI-H).
The role of adjuvant immunotherapy for those who received neoadjuvant immunotherapy is not defined. Enrollment in a clinical trial is encouraged.

nodes.criteria: T any, N1-2

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy recommended. The role of adjuvant immunotherapy for those who received neoadjuvant immunotherapy is not defined. Enrollment in a clinical trial is encouraged.

management_pathways.title: Metastatic Adenocarcinoma and/or Serial Biomarker Elevation

nodes.criteria: Metastatic adenocarcinoma at presentation

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative intent. Multidisciplinary evaluation for potential metastasectomy in select cases of limited visceral/peritoneal metastases (rare).

nodes.criteria: Serial biomarker elevation (CEA/CA19-9)

options.label: Physical examination, endoscopic evaluation(s), chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast
Consider FDG-PET/CT if CT evaluation is negative
Reevaluate biomarker and CT with contrast in 3 months if initial workup negative

adjuvant_logic.summary: If workup positive for recurrence/metastasis, manage as metastatic adenocarcinoma.

items.text: Chest, abdomen, pelvis CT with IV and oral contrast
items.recommendation: Initial workup/staging for all patients. Evaluate local tumor extent, lymph nodes, liver, peritoneum, lungs. Chest CT does not require contrast. [SBA-1, SBA-A]

items.text: Abdominal/pelvic MRI with IV gadolinium-based contrast
items.recommendation: May be used instead of CT if IV iodinated contrast is contraindicated. Also for further evaluation of indeterminate liver lesions. [SBA-A]
May be considered when primary tumor is poorly visualized by standard methods. Not routinely indicated for monitoring. [SBA-A]

Not indicated for routine initial staging or surveillance. May be considered in instances of equivocal CT or MR results, particularly for evaluation of potential peritoneal disease where lesions are sized greater than lower limits of FDG-PET detection. [SBA-1, SBA-6, SBA-7, SBA-A]
Recommended for detection and pathologic sampling when duodenal malignancy is suspected. May include palliative stenting if obstruction detected. [SBA-1, SBA-A]

Useful for enhanced pre-therapeutic clinical staging of proximal small intestinal malignancies and to discern duodenal from ampullary, biliary, or pancreatic primaries. Should be considered when needed for clinical staging. [SBA-1, SBA-A]

items.text: Push- or device-assisted enteroscopy (double- or single-balloon)
items.recommendation: Not required for routine staging workup. May be considered in patients with small intestinal strictures for diagnostic and/or palliative benefit. Biopsy may be performed. [SBA-A]

Consider when radiographic imaging and other forms of endoscopy fail to reveal a suspected primary lesion. Not the preferred primary method for diagnostic workup due to inability to obtain tissue. Contraindicated where small bowel obstruction or strictures exist. [SBA-A]

Prefer pathologic confirmation of disease prior to resection, where possible. Reporting should include primary site, grade, T stage, number of LN evaluated/positive, margin status, lymphovascular invasion, MSI/MMR status, evidence of celiac/Crohn's disease. [SBA-1, SBA-B]

items.text: Mismatch repair (MMR) or microsatellite instability (MSI) testing
items.recommendation: Universal MMR or MSI testing is recommended in all newly diagnosed patients with SBA. Important prognostic and/or predictive biomarker. Patients with dMMR/MSI-H may have improved survival in stage II disease. [SBA-1, SBA-B]

items.text: Biomarker testing for metastatic disease (performed as rapidly as possible)
items.recommendation: Include: RAS and BRAF V600E mutations; HER2 (ERBB2) overexpression/amplification; MMR/MSI status (if not previously done). Testing should be conducted as part of multigene panel testing (MGPT), which would identify rare and actionable mutations and gene fusions such as POLE/POLD1, RET, NTRK 1/2/3, and tumor mutational burden (TMB). [SBA-8, SBA-D 1 of 12]
Recommended for initial staging and for surveillance. CEA and CA 19-9 every 3-6 mo for 2 y, then every 6 mo for a total of 5 y. [SBA-1, SBA-6, SBA-7]

items.text: Consider studies for celiac disease or inflammatory bowel disease
items.recommendation: Depending on tumor location and patient history, celiac disease or Crohn's disease may need to be assessed. [SBA-1, SBA-B]

items.text: Counseling for familial malignancies and risk assessment
items.recommendation: All patients with SBA should be counseled for familial malignancies and considered for risk assessment, including Lynch syndrome, FAP, and other polypoid mutations. Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High Risk Assessment: Colorectal, Endometrial, and Gastric. [SBA-1, MS-3, MS-4]

items.text: Assessment of social determinants of health
items.recommendation: Refer to NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List. [SBA-1, footnote c]

surgery.role: Surgical resection is the primary treatment for localized (Stage I-III) SBA and may be considered for select patients with metastatic disease (metastasectomy). The goal is complete (R0) resection with adequate lymph node dissection.
principles: Intraoperative staging of the abdomen, particularly including the mesentery, omentum, and peritoneum, should be completed in all cases.
Adequate lymph node dissection, consisting of the resection and evaluation of at least eight lymph nodes, should be the goal for all resections.

Diagnostic laparoscopy is not routinely indicated for clearly resectable early-stage disease. It may be considered when occult peritoneal or metastatic disease is suspected—such as in locally advanced tumors, high-risk histology, elevated tumor markers, or discordant findings—and when results would alter management.

procedures.name: Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple)
procedures.approach: Open or minimally invasive (by experienced surgeons).
procedures.indication: Should be considered for all duodenal malignancies, particularly if arising in the second portion of the duodenum or invading any portion of the ampulla or pancreas. Pylorus preservation is acceptable in the absence of a hereditary condition.
procedures.key_complications: Pancreatic fistula, delayed gastric emptying, bleeding, infection.

procedures.name: Limited Segmentectomy (Duodenum)
procedures.indication: Controversial. May be considered for select cases in the absence of a hereditary condition, particularly in lesions on the anti-mesenteric side of the intestine that involve the third and fourth segments of the duodenum. Case reports suggest it may be considered for lesions of the first portion, particularly those <2 cm in size and located on the mesenteric side.
procedures.key_complications: May have lower lymph node yield; potential for positive margins if not carefully performed.

procedures.name: Segmentectomy (Jejunum/Ileum)
procedures.indication: Standard for jejunal and ileal tumors.
procedures.key_complications: Standard surgical risks (bleeding, infection, anastomotic leak).

procedures.name: Terminal Ileal Resection with Right Hemicolectomy
procedures.indication: Should be used for distal ileal tumors.

surgery.resectability_criteria: The decision for resection is based on achieving an R0 resection while maintaining bowel continuity and function when possible. Tumors are considered resectable if complete removal with negative margins is feasible without causing unacceptable morbidity. Locally advanced tumors may be considered for neoadjuvant therapy with the goal of conversion to resectability. Diagnostic laparoscopy may be used to assess for occult metastatic disease that would preclude curative resection.
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Not explicitly detailed in the source text beyond multidisciplinary evaluation and the potential use of neoadjuvant therapy for locally advanced disease to achieve resectability.

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) has a limited role in SBA. Its use is primarily considered for duodenal adenocarcinoma in specific situations: for margin-positive (R1/Rx) resections (adjuvant setting) or for patients with locally advanced disease who remain unresectable after induction chemotherapy (neoadjuvant setting). RT is not generally indicated for jejunal or ileal tumors.
principles: The use of RT requires careful patient selection and should be evaluated by multidisciplinary teams at high-volume centers.
Fluoropyrimidine-based chemotherapy should be delivered concurrently with RT.
Advanced treatment planning (IMRT) should be considered to limit toxicity to adjacent normal organs.
Image-guided RT (IGRT) should be routinely used during treatment with IMRT.

dose_frameworks.name: Concurrent Chemoradiation (Duodenum)
dose_frameworks.indication: For margin-positive duodenal cancer post-resection (adjuvant) or for locally advanced disease after induction chemotherapy (neoadjuvant).

approaches.name: Adjuvant Chemoradiation for Duodenal Cancer
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Capecitabine 825 mg/m2 PO twice daily Monday-Friday on days of radiation treatment only, or fluorouracil 225 mg/m2 IV over 24 hours (continuous infusion) daily.
approaches.indication: For patients with duodenal adenocarcinoma who have positive margins (R1/Rx) following resection. Not recommended for all resected duodenal cancers as database analysis shows no survival benefit versus chemotherapy alone.
approaches.key_trial: Not specified for SBA-specific efficacy; based on extrapolation from CRC and retrospective SBA data [Ecker BL, et al. Cancer 2017; Kelsey CR, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2007].
approaches.toxicities: GI (nausea, diarrhea, enteritis), hematologic, fatigue, potential for late bowel toxicity.

approaches.name: Neoadjuvant Chemoradiation for Unresectable Duodenal Cancer
approaches.indication: For patients with locally advanced duodenal adenocarcinoma who remain unresectable after a course of induction chemotherapy.
approaches.key_trial: Small prospective trials in duodenal/pancreatic adenocarcinomas showed high resection rates after neoadjuvant chemoradiation [Yeung RS, et al. Cancer 1993; Coia L, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1994].
approaches.toxicities: As above, plus potential for increased perioperative morbidity.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is used in the adjuvant setting for resected high-risk Stage II and Stage III disease, and as primary treatment for locally unresectable or metastatic SBA. Treatment recommendations are largely extrapolated from colorectal cancer (CRC) data. Participation in clinical trials is especially encouraged. The choice of regimen depends on performance status, comorbidities, prior therapy, and tumor biomarker status (especially MMR/MSI, BRAF, HER2, NTRK, RET, KRAS G12C, TMB). Many regimens are Category 2A.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: FOLFOX or CAPEOX for 3-6 months (IDEA trial extrapolation for duration). Fluorouracil/Leucovorin or capecitabine for 6 months are alternatives.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on retrospective SBA data and extrapolation from CRC adjuvant trials (IDEA). No patients with SBA were included in the IDEA pooled analysis, but noninferior 3-year DFS with 3 months of CAPEOX vs 6 months is extrapolated.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Observation may be appropriate for select Stage II patients. Sequential chemoradiation may be considered for margin-positive duodenal cancers.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: pMMR/MSS status. High-risk features for Stage II guide decision.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: For T4,N0 or N+ disease: FOLFOX + atezolizumab or CAPEOX + atezolizumab. FOLFOX or CAPEOX alone are alternatives.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on emerging data for dMMR/MSI-H tumors and extrapolation from CRC immunotherapy trials (e.g., ATOMIC trial).
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Observation may be appropriate for Stage I and Stage II (T1-3,N0) disease.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype (TMB>50 mut/Mb).

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Intensive therapy: FOLFOX or CAPEOX ± bevacizumab, FOLFIRI ± bevacizumab, FOLFIRINOX ± bevacizumab. Non-intensive therapy: Capecitabine or fluorouracil/leucovorin ± bevacizumab. If BRAF V600E mutation: (FOLFOX/encorafenib + cetuximab or panitumumab). If tissue TMB-high (>10 mut/Mb): pembrolizumab (category 2A).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on phase II trials in SBA (e.g., ORR ~50% for FOLFOX/CAPEOX) and extrapolation from CRC.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Taxane-based chemotherapy. For patients who had FOLFOX/CAPEOX in adjuvant setting within past 12 months: FOLFIRI ± bevacizumab, taxane-based, or BRAF V600E-directed therapy.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: BRAF V600E, HER2, NTRK, RET, KRAS G12C, TMB, MMR/MSI testing recommended for all metastatic patients.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Second-Line and Subsequent Therapy for Metastatic Disease (pMMR/MSS)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: CAPEOX, FOLFIRI, FOLFOX, irinotecan, or taxane-based chemotherapy ± bevacizumab (if not previously given). Biomarker-directed options: BRAF V600E (encorafenib + cetuximab/panitumumab, or dabrafenib/trametinib, or FOLFOX/encorafenib + cetuximab/panitumumab), HER2 overexpression/amplification (fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki or pertuzumab/trastuzumab or tucatinib/trastuzumab), NTRK fusion (entrectinib, larotrectinib, repotrectinib), RET fusion (selpercatinib), KRAS G12C (sotorasib or adagrasib ± cetuximab/panitumumab). If tissue TMB-high (>10 mut/Mb): pembrolizumab.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Extrapolated from CRC and basket trials. Some agents (e.g., fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki) have specific data in SBA.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Any Line of Therapy (dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, dostarlimab-gxly, cemiplimab-rwlc, retifanlimab-dlwr, tislelizumab-jsgr, toripalimab-tpzi, penpulimab-kcqx, atezolizumab, avelumab, cosibelimab-ipdl, durvalumab) or combination (nivolumab + ipilimumab).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on high response rates to checkpoint inhibitors in dMMR/MSI-H tumors (e.g., pembrolizumab ORR 42% in SBA, KEYNOTE-158).
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Chemotherapy regimens as for pMMR/MSS disease.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype (TMB>50 mut/Mb).

key_regimens.name: FOLFOX

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus, then 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 Days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46-48 Hours) continuous infusion

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Peripheral neuropathy, myelosuppression, mucositis, diarrhea, nausea/vomiting, fatigue. Discontinuation of oxaliplatin after 3-4 months should be strongly considered to limit neurotoxicity.

key_regimens.name: CAPEOX
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Peripheral neuropathy, hand-foot syndrome, myelosuppression, diarrhea, nausea/vomiting, fatigue. DPYD testing recommended prior to capecitabine initiation.

key_regimens.name: FOLFOX + Atezolizumab

drugs.name: Atezolizumab
drugs.day: Every 2 weeks for 12 cycles (6 months), then maintenance 840 mg every 2 weeks for 13 cycles (total 12 months)

key_regimens.cycle_days: Atezolizumab cycles aligned with FOLFOX cycles. Total adjuvant therapy duration: 12 months.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) plus FOLFOX toxicities.

key_regimens.name: CAPEOX + Atezolizumab

drugs.name: Atezolizumab
drugs.day: Every 3 weeks for 8 cycles (6 months), then maintenance 1200 mg every 3 weeks for 8 cycles (total 12 months)

key_regimens.cycle_days: Atezolizumab cycles aligned with CAPEOX cycles. Total adjuvant therapy duration: 12 months.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) plus CAPEOX toxicities.

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab (for dMMR/MSI-H or TMB-high)
key_regimens.setting: Any line for dMMR/MSI-H or TMB-high (>10 mut/Mb)

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 200 mg IV every 3 weeks OR 400 mg IV every 6 weeks OR 4 mg/kg IV every 6 weeks

key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat as above until progression or unacceptable toxicity.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events (pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, etc.).

key_regimens.name: Encorafenib + Cetuximab or Panitumumab (for BRAF V600E)
key_regimens.setting: Second-line and subsequent for BRAF V600E
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Encorafenib: arthralgia, fatigue, diarrhea. Cetuximab/Panitumumab: acneiform rash, hypomagnesemia, infusion reactions.

key_regimens.name: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (for HER2 overexpression IHC 3+)
key_regimens.setting: Second-line and subsequent for HER2 overexpression (IHC 3+)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat every 3 weeks until progression or unacceptable toxicity.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Nausea, anemia, diarrhea, fatigue, vomiting, interstitial lung disease (ILD/pneumonitis). May not be indicated in patients with underlying lung issues due to risk of drug-related ILD deaths.

treatment_response_assessment.title: Assessment of Treatment Response
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response assessment timing is not explicitly detailed in the source text for SBA-specific regimens. General oncologic principles apply: during systemic therapy for metastatic disease, response is typically assessed every 2-3 months (6-9 weeks) using imaging. For neoadjuvant therapy, restaging should occur after a course of induction therapy to assess for resectability.
response_logic: For metastatic disease treated with systemic therapy, standard RECIST criteria are used to assess response (complete response, partial response, stable disease, progressive disease).
If response is observed, continuation of therapy until progression or unacceptable toxicity is standard.
If progression occurs, reassess biomarker status if not recently done and consider next-line therapy based on molecular profile and prior treatments.
imaging_recommendations: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast is the primary modality for response assessment.
FDG-PET/CT is not indicated for routine response assessment but may be used for equivocal findings.
Endoscopic evaluation may be used for local assessment of duodenal tumors.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Re-biopsy at the time of progression is not routinely mandated but may be considered if there is concern for histologic transformation or to obtain tissue for molecular profiling if not previously done.

For local recurrence after curative-intent surgery, the patient should be reassessed by a multidisciplinary team. Salvage options may include re-resection (if feasible), chemoradiation (for duodenal tumors), or systemic therapy.
For oligometastatic progression, reassessment for metastasectomy or ablative therapies may be considered in select cases with limited metastatic burden.
